coredata.dc:title: A graphene quantum dots based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Guangming
Wang, Yang
Tang, Yun
Zhao, Dan
Guo, Qingfu
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for ultrasensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was developed using signal amplification strategy based on poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (P5FIn/erGO) and Au nanoparticle (AuNP) decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@AuNP). As an effective matrix for immobilization of primary antibody (Ab1), P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite facilitated the ion transport during the redox reactions and provided larger surface areas for the immobilization of Ab1. GQDs@AuNP was used as labels to conjugate with secondary antibody (Ab2), which improved electron transfer capability with stable ECL intensity. The multiple amplification of P5FIn/erGO and GQDs@AuNP made the ECL immunosensor have a broad linear range from 0.1pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 and a low detection limit with 3.78fgmL−1. In addition, this ECL immunosensor performed with admirable stability and good selectivity and reproducibility as well. When this immunosensor was used for the analysis of CEA in human serum, good recoveries were obtained. Thus, there will be a promising future in the early diagnosis of cancer to detect CEA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Immunosensor
Graphene quantum dots
Graphene oxide
Polyindole
Carcinoembryonic antigen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, AuNP and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite
ECL detection of the fabricated immunosensor
Characterization of GQDs, erGO, ECL probe and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite
The ECL response of the ECL immunosensor
Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the ECL immunosensor
Clinical application of the ECL immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51373089 ), NSF of Shandong ( ZR2011BM003 ), Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20123719120006 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs, SEM images of (C) erGO and (D) P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite.
(A) CV and (B) EIS of stepwise fabrication process of the immune modified electrodes (scan rate: 100mVs−1).
ECL signals of the immunosensor for detection of CEA at different concentrations (a–f: 10, 1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001ngmL−1). Inset: The calibration plot between ECL intensity and lg c[CEA].

(A) The stability of ECL immunosensor under consecutive cyclic potential scans for 15 cycles, (B) the storage stability of ECL immunosensor in the presence of CEA (0.1ngmL−1) evaluated every 5 days, (C) five electrodes to test the same concentration of CEA (0.1ngmL−1), (D) interfering effects of sample matrix components on ECL intensity of ECL immunosensor.
The fabrication progress of GQDs based ECL immunosensor based on P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: A graphene quantum dots based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for ultrasensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was developed using signal amplification strategy based on poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (P5FIn/erGO) and Au nanoparticle (AuNP) decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@AuNP). As an effective matrix for immobilization of primary antibody (Ab1), P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite facilitated the ion transport during the redox reactions and provided larger surface areas for the immobilization of Ab1. GQDs@AuNP was used as labels to conjugate with secondary antibody (Ab2), which improved electron transfer capability with stable ECL intensity. The multiple amplification of P5FIn/erGO and GQDs@AuNP made the ECL immunosensor have a broad linear range from 0.1pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 and a low detection limit with 3.78fgmL−1. In addition, this ECL immunosensor performed with admirable stability and good selectivity and reproducibility as well. When this immunosensor was used for the analysis of CEA in human serum, good recoveries were obtained. Thus, there will be a promising future in the early diagnosis of cancer to detect CEA.

A sandwich-type ECL immunosensor based on P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was developed for CEA detection using GQDs@AuNP nanocomposite as the ECL probe. This ECL immunosensor had good stability, reproducibility and specificity.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Now cancer incidence and mortality are increasing all over the world and become the major public health problem (Chen et al., 2016; Wu and Qu, 2015a). Tumor markers as molecules found in blood and body tissues can be elevated due to the presence of one or more types of cancer (Chang et al., 2016). In order to realize the early diagnosis of cancer, the sensitive determination of tumor markers is of great importance (Miyake et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2015). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is one of the most extensively used tumor markers which expresses in many malignancies (Huang et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016a). Up to now, many kinds of immunoassay techniques for the CEA detection have been developed such as electrochemical immunoassay (Wang et al., 2016b), fluoroimmunoassay (Wu et al., 2015b), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) assay (Liu et al., 2016) and so on. For example, Feng's group (Feng et al., 2016) has developed a sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for CEA by using Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 as carriers and gold nanoparticles-graphene oxide (GO-AuNP) as the platform. Our group (Zhao et al., 2016a) has described an electrochemical immunosensor based on the nanocomposite consisting of electrochemically reduced graphene oxide and poly (indole-6-carboxylic acid) for CEA detection.

With the development of technology, ECL assay is the most noteworthy due to the various advantages including high sensitivity, low background signal and fast sample analysis in the sensitive determination of CEA (Zhao et al., 2016b). Thus, in order to obtain the sensitive detection of cancer biomarkers, sandwiched-type ECL immunosensors have received much attention due to the use of a couple of match antibodies and the secondary antibody conjugated with different labels for signal amplification (Zhou et al., 2016). Wang's group (Li et al., 2017) has fabricated a sandwiched ECL immunosensor for the detection of CEA based on poly(ethylenimine) functionalized graphene oxide modified with CQDs (PEI-GO-CQDs) and AuNP with a detection limit of 1.67pgmL−1, which provides a promising potential for the detection of trace amount of CEA in clinical diagnosis.

As ECL luminophores, Ru(bpy)3 2+, cadmium-containing quantum dots (QDs), and carbon dots (C-dots) have been extensively studied in recent years. Despite many nanomaterials have been employed as the labels, it is still a challenge to find new designed nanocomposites which can improve the simplicity and sensitivity of ECL immunosensor. As a new promising material in carbon family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently been subjected to extensive research for biomedical applications (Kumar et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015). GQDs have graphene structure with single or multilayers of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms packed into a unique planar structure, which makes it an ideal platform of biomedical applications. Compared with other luminophores, GQDs have low cost, large specific surface area, unique electro-optical properties, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness, and attractive biocompatibility partly arising from their quantum confinement and zigzag edge effects (Li et al., 2011; Lou et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2014a). Especially, as an ECL probe, the attractive merits of GQDs include more stable ECL and onset potential closer to 0V presumably due to accelerated electron transport by high content of sp2 carbon compared with C-dots (Zheng et al., 2015). Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) could drastically alter electronic characteristics of GQDs and offer more active sites through doping N atoms. However, it is difficult to form a continuous N-GQDs film and effectively bridge the N-GQDs with the glassy carbon electrode (GE) compared with GQDs as ECL probe (Li et al., 2012). GQDs have been used to fabricate ECL sensor, such as the ECL sensor for detection of Cd2+ and Pb2+ (Dong et al., 2014), which become promising to substitute traditional QDs as ECL luminophores. Moreover, owing to its small size, GQDs were shown to be more compatible with the biological system due to its characteristic planar surface for π-π* interactions. Although valuable, little attention has been paid to GQDs-based ECL immunosensor for CEA detection.

Moreover, in order to improve the sensitivity of CEA detection, electrode modified material is another critical factor for suitable carrier's immobilization and signal amplification. Conducting polymers (CPs), as excellent sensing materials, have been extensively studied due to their high sensitivities, good conductivity and low cost properties (Hatchett and Josowicz, 2008). CPs with reactive functionalities can not only act as suitable immobilization carriers for biomolecules, but also have active effects on the transduction of biosensors (Jang et al., 2005). Among CPs, polyindole families have been investigated due to its several advantages, such as good photoelectric property, high redox activity and good stability (Nie et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2014). Poly(5-formylindole) (P5FIn) film with good nanostructure can be obtained directly by electropolymerization (Nie et al., 2013). The introduction of aldehyde groups can improve the optoelectronic properties of the materials, which also extends their applications in the biosensing or drug delivery realms. In addition, graphene oxide (GO) is water-soluble nanomaterial, which is a graphene sheet containing functional groups (Liu et al., 2011). Electrochemically reduced graphene oxide (erGO) can be prepared by electrochemical reduction of GO. This amazing planar sheet of carbon material has been used in many fields, such as the application of sensors. Hybridization of a conducting polymer with GO and graphene has been studied and demonstrated enhancement in sensitivity and selectivity of sensors compared with pure GO and graphene sensors (Salavagione et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2014b). It will be worthy to obtain poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide (P5FIn/erGO) nanocomposite because this novel nanocomposite can improve the conductivity and provide a large surface area to immobilize biomolecules. It may be also a good candidate for the modified electrode material of sandwich-type ECL immunosensor.

In this study, a sandwich-type ECL immunosensor based on a new GQDs-nanosystem and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was developed for ultrasensitive detection of CEA. The complex Au nanoparticle (AuNP) decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@AuNP) was used to immobilize secondary antibody (Ab2) as ECL probe and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was the platform to immobilize primary antibody (Ab1). This sandwich-type ECL immunosensor has a wide linear range from 0.1pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 3.78fgmL−1. This study provided a new method for CEA detection, which showed great potential application in the clinical diagnosis and practice.

Graphene oxide (GO, > 99wt%) was purchased from Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). CEA, primary antibody (Ab1) and secondary antibody (Ab2) were obtained from Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The other details are provided in Supplementary information.

ECL emission was detected with an MPI-A ECL analyzer (Xi’An, Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd., Xi’An, China). Scanning electronmicroscopy (SEM) measurements were taken by using a JEOL JSM-6700F scanning electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The other details are displayed in Supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, AuNP and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized according to the previous literature (Dong et al., 2012) with the modification. The AuNP was prepared according to the previously reported literature (Handley, 1989) (Fig. S1). P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was obtained by electrochemical reduction of GO at a constant potential of − 1.3V vs. Ag/AgCl for 200s (Yang et al., 2013) and then the electrodeposition of P5FIn on the erGO/GE was carried out in the electrolyte of ACN containing 0.05molL−1 5FIn monomer and 0.1molL−1 tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBATFB). The other details are provided in Supplementary information.

As shown in Scheme 1A, 30μL GQDs and 120μL AuNP were dissolved in 1mL 0.01molL−1 phosphate buffer saline (PBS). 100μL of the mixed solution was mixed with 100μL of 0.1mgmL−1 Ab2 (0.01molL−1 PB, pH 7.4), followed by incubation at 4°C for 2h and then diluted with 0.01molL−1 PBS three times. For blocking of non-specific binding sites, 100μL of 2wt% bovine serum albumin (BSA) was added into the solution obtained and incubated at 4°C for another 4h. The resulting conjugates were denoted as ECL probe. Scheme 1B outlined the fabricating steps of the modified electrode material. To begin with, GE was carefully polished successively and then GO solution was dipped onto GE. After electrochemical reduction, erGO/GE was obtained. Then electrochemical polymerization was carried out, and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was modified on the working electrode.

Scheme 1 described the fabrication steps of the ECL immunosensor in detail. After the modification of the working electrode, 20μL AuNP was dropped onto the electrode and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 10μL 0.1mgmL−1 Ab1 (0.01molL−1, pH 7.4) was dropped on the electrode surface, and then, the electrode was incubated for 1h at 37°C to get Ab1/AuNP/P5FIn/erGO/GE. Next, this modified electrode was incubated in BSA (2wt%) solution for 1h at 37°C to block the non-specific binding sites and BSA/Ab1/AuNP/P5FIn/erGO/GE was prepared. Following that, the modified electrode was incubated in different concentrations of CEA solution at 37°C for another 50min. Finally, 10μL ECL probe solution was dropped on CEA/BSA/Ab1/AuNP/P5FIn/erGO/GE for immunoreaction at 37°C for 50min. The obtained sandwich-type ECL immunosensor was stored at 4°C. According to the reported literatures (Li et al., 2017a), the ECL mechanism (Scheme 1) may be illustrated as follows:
(1) GQDs + e -
→ GQDs - •
(2) S2O8 2- + e -
→ S2O8 2-•
(3) S2O8 2-• → SO4 2- + SO4 -•
(4) GQDs - • + SO4 -• → GQDs* + SO4 2-
(5) GQDs* → GQDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of the fabricated immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL measurements were performed in a detector cell containing 5mL Tris-HCl (0.05molL−1, pH 7.4) with 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 and 0.1molL−1 KCl. The ECL intensity was gained by a MPI-A ECL analyzer with working potential from − 1.65 to 0V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 100mVs−1, and the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) at 800V.

CV is a valid and visualized method for investigating the reversibility and electroactivity of the nanocomposite. The CV of erGO/GE were measured in ACN containing 0.1molL−1 TBATFB and 0.05molL−1 5FIn monomer. P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite showed a large current density on the first curve in Fig. S2A. With the CV scan continued, the gradual increase in the redox couple currents implied that amounts of polymer was deposited on the erGO/GE. In the process of electropolymerization, P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite exhibited wide redox peaks due to the long polymer chain and wide distribution (Randriamahazaka et al., 2007). P5FIn/erGO/GE showed a lower anodic oxidation potential (0.9V vs. Ag/AgCl) than P5FIn/GE (1.1V vs. Ag/AgCl) in the inset of Fig. S2A, which indicated that P5FIn/erGO/GE had better electrical property. The CV comparison of P5FIn/GE and P5FIn/erGO/GE of the tenth cycle was shown in Fig. S2B. P5FIn/erGO/GE exhibited larger peak current density and wider peak potential, which indicated that the erGO improved the electron transfer ability (Yang et al., 2010) and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite had better electrochemical activity than P5FIn. All these phenomena demonstrated that P5FIn/erGO film can be an outstanding electrode modification material and will be used for the fabrication of the ECL immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, erGO, ECL probe and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared GQDs solution remains homogeneous even after 3 months at room temperature without any perceptible changes. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the as-prepared GQDs were shown in Fig. 1A. It can be seen that GQDs are monodisperse, and the average diameter is about 20nm (Fig. 1A). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the prepared GQDs (Fig. 1B) showed that the synthesized GQDs exhibited an evident absorption peak located at 356nm. The results indicated that the GQDs were successfully prepared. UV–vis absorption spectra was also employed for demonstration of P5FIn/erGO and ECL probe formation (Figs. S3 and S4). The detailed explanations were provided in Supplementary information.

Fig. 1C and D showed the SEM images of the morphology and microstructure of erGO and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1C, erGO has a lamellar structure with slight folds on the surface. In Fig. 1D, P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite had a nanosheet structure which was homogeneous and intact fiber-shaped, indicating a well-dispersedness and incorporation of erGO in the composite layer. Simultaneously, this kind of structure improved electrochemical properties of the composite material according to the cyclic voltammetry investigation in this work (Fig. S2). The homogeneous and intact fiber-shape can facilitate the ion transport during the redox reactions and provide larger surface areas for the immobilization of Ab1. All these superiorities of the P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite material makes it an ideal candidate for the fabrication of a CPs based biosensor.

The fabrication process of the immune modified electrodes was electrochemical characterized by CV measurements. Fig. 2A was the CV of the stepwise fabrication process recorded in 0.1molL−1 phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) during working voltage of 0.2–1.6V vs. Ag/AgCl. As can be seen from Fig. 2A, after the electropolymerization of P5FIn on the erGO/GE, a set of redox peaks were presented, which implied that P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite had good electrochemical activity. It's worth noting that the peak potential of P5FIn oxidation (~ 1.25V) was larger than that in Fig. S2 (~ 1.0V), which may be due to different electrolyte solutions. The difference of the peak potential of oxidation peak was related to the kinetics of the doping-dedoping reaction, which maybe attribute to the different doping anions (Maarouf et al., 1994; Tian et al., 2008; Ming et al., 2016). When AuNP was modified on the P5FIn/erGO/GE, there was an obvious increase of the peak current due to the amplified action of AuNP. AuNP played the part of electrical channel or conductor in the electrochemical reaction, and consequently promoted the electron transfer process (Ambrosi et al., 2007). The peak current decreased obviously after successfully coated with Ab1, BSA and CEA onto the electrode, which was due to the blocking effect of insulated protein molecules on the electron transfer process (Gao et al., 2016). After GQDs@AuNP-Ab2 was linked to the electrode, the peak current increased compared with the CEA/BSA/Ab1/AuNP/P5FIn/erGO/GE, which was attributed to the high conductivity of AuNP (Jie et al., 2008) and the small particle size of GQDs (Dong et al., 2015). These results indicated that the immunosensing substrate was formed successfully and can be used in the proposed immunoassay.

The fabrication of the immunosensor was also monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS was recorded in 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1molL−1 KCl. As shown in Fig. 2B, compared with P5FIn/erGO modified electrode (curve a), the diameter of the semicircular domain decreased after the electrode was modified with AuNP (curve b), which was due to the excellent electrical conductivity of AuNP. This result indicated that AuNP layer was successfully formed on the surface of modified electrode. When the modified electrode was conjugated with Ab1 and CEA (curve c and d), the diameter of the semicircular domain increased gradually due to insulation property of protein. After GQDs@AuNP-Ab2 was linked to the modified electrode, the diameter of semicircular domain decreased obviously compared with the CEA/Ab1/AuNP/P5FIn/erGO/GE, which was attributed to the high conductivity of GQDs (curve e). Therefore, these results demonstrated that this immunosensor was successfully fabricated.

The following parameters of detection conditions were optimized: (A) excitation potential; (B) sample pH value; (C) incubation temperature of ECL probe; (D) incubation time of ECL probe; (E) GQDs concentration of ECL probe. The corresponding respective data and figures were shown in Fig. S5. According to Fig. S5, the above experimental conditions were found to give best results as follows: (A) the excitation potential was at − 1.65V; (B) a sample pH value was 7.4; (C) optimal incubation temperature of ECL probe was 37°C; (D) incubation time of ECL probe was 50min; (E) the GQDs concentration of ECL probe was 0.8mgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL response of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions mentioned above, Fig. 3 provided the ECL intensities of the immune electrodes for CEA detection in different concentrations. The ECL intensity increased linearly with the logarithm value of CEA concentration ranging from 0.1pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1. The regression equation was Y = 14008.60 + 2379.31lg c[CEA] with a correlation coefficient of 0.9957. The detection limit was calculated as 3.78fgmL−1 based on the calculation formula LoD = 3σ × c min/k. The σ was estimated by measuring replicates of a blank sample for 11 times and calculating the standard deviation (SD). The c min was the minimum concentration of the linear range. The k was the slope of the regression equation. Such low detection limit indicated good sensitivity of this ECL immunosensor (Armbruster and Pry, 2008). The large surface area of the nanometer-sized GQDs and integration of P5FIn/erGO provided great active sites for the CEA, leading to a low detection limit.

Table S1 provided the comparison of analytical performance of this immunosensor with other methods. According to this comparation, the detection limit of this work was the lowest. The high sensitivity was mainly due to the following aspects: (1) GQDs had unique electro-optical property, the complex GQDs@AuNP improved electron transfer capability and showed a stable ECL intensity. (2) P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite exhibited good conductivity and supported a large surface area to immobilize more AuNP. (3) AuNP can accelerate the electron transfer ability and enhance the electrochemical response. This strategy will be promising for the development of effective immunosensors with high performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of this ECL immunosensor was studied by detecting ECL intensity under consecutive cyclic potential scans for 15 cycles at 0.1ngmL−1 of CEA. The constant steady signals were shown in Fig. 4A and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.28%. This result indicated that the modified electrode possessed admirable stability. The storage stability of this immunosensor was tested by monitoring its ECL response. It can be found in Fig. 4B that the ECL intensity decreased slightly after five days, ten days and fifteen days, and it showed the stability after fifteen days. The corresponding RSD was calculated at 7.5%. These results indicated the stability of the immunosensor was acceptable. The reproducibility of the immunosensor was tested by using five fabricated immunosensors incubated with 0.1ngmL−1 CEA. The relatively close ECL responses of the prepared electrodes were shown in Fig. 4C and the RSD was 3.3%, which was satisfactory for detection of CEA. To further investigate the selectivity and specificity of the proposed ECL immunosensor, the contrast experiments were performed by incubating with interfering antigen. As shown in Fig. 4D, there was weaker ECL response closed to the result of the blank test when the assays were carried out in the presence of 0.1ngmL−1 AFP, cholesterol (Chol), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu) and human serum albumin (HSA), respectively. On the other hand, when the ECL response was tested in the mixture containing CEA, AFP, Chol, UA, Glu and HSA (each concentration is 0.1ngmL−1), no remarkable difference was found compared with the ECL response obtained from 0.1ngmL−1 CEA only. Thus, this proposed ECL immunosensor had satisfactory selectivity and high specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clinical application of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the practical applicability and reliability of the proposed ECL system, this ECL immunosensor was used to measure real human serum samples from hospital. Since the CEA concentration of the sample (40ngmL−1 CEA) was much higher than the upper limit of the linear range of this sensor, the sample was diluted with 0.01molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.15molL−1 NaCl before assay in order to obtain the different concentrations of standard CEA. Tests were performed by adding the immunosensor into five different concentrations of CEA, each sample was detected three times. The experimental results were shown in Table S2, the RSD was ranging from 1.2% to 3.6% and the average recoveries of the three measurements were ranging from 98.6% to 101.1%, which indicated that the proposed immunosensor is available to apply for detecting CEA in human serum.

A sandwich-type ECL immunosensor based on P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was developed for CEA detection using GQDs@AuNP nanocomposite as the ECL probe. GQDs@AuNP nanocomposite took the advantages of high ECL intensity of GQDs, good conductivity and fast electrostatic transportation of AuNP. GQDs and P5FIn/erGO provided great active sites for the CEA due to the large surface area. The obtained immunosensor exhibited a broad linear range from 0.1pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 and a low detection limit with 3.78fgmL−1 for the ultrasensitive detection of CEA. The results indicated the proposed ECL immunosensor presented good stability, reproducibility and specificity. The ECL immunosensor provides a promising potential application to detect CEA in the early diagnosis of cancer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51373089), NSF of Shandong (ZR2011BM003), Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123719120006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.10.021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methods for synthesis of colloidal gold
Colloidal Gold: Principles, Methods, and Applications
